title: American Literature
intro: American Literature is one of the two pillars of the quizbowl literature distribution, alongside British Literature, and typically claims a fifth or more of all literature questions at every level of play. Its scope runs from Puritan devotional verse and colonial-era poetry through the postmodern doorstop novels of the late twentieth century and the Pulitzer regulars of the present day, and it embraces every form the category recognizes: lyric and epic poetry, the novel and short story, drama, and a distinctively American tradition of essayistic nonfiction running from Emerson through the New Journalism.

The shape of the canon is visible in the frequency data. Emily Dickinson is the single most-asked answerline in the subcategory, and poets are strikingly dominant at the top of the table: Edgar Allan Poe, William Carlos Williams, Walt Whitman, Wallace Stevens, Edna St. Vincent Millay, and Robert Frost all rank alongside or ahead of most novelists. The prose canon clusters into recognizable movements that writers of study guides and packets alike treat as units — the American Renaissance of Hawthorne and Melville, Gilded Age realism and naturalism, the Lost Generation and high modernism, the Southern Gothic school around Faulkner and Flannery O'Connor, the Harlem Renaissance and the broader African American tradition running from Frederick Douglass to Toni Morrison, and the postwar line from Salinger and Bellow through Pynchon, DeLillo, and David Foster Wallace.

Questions take several distinct answerline forms, all reflected in this table. Author answerlines are the most common, but famous works are asked directly and often independently of their authors — A Raisin in the Sun, Beloved, and The Iceman Cometh all out-rank many canonical authors — and the same underlying topic can surface under multiple answerline spellings (Phillis and Phyllis Wheatley, W. H. and Wystan Hugh Auden, Kurt Vonnegut with and without the Jr.). Character answerlines like Jim from Huckleberry Finn appear, as does a healthy population of common-link answers — cities, states, wars, and even first names — that stitch together clues from many different writers and reward breadth over depth.

Two skews are worth noting. Drama is unusually prominent for a national literature — American drama effectively holds its own slot in most distributions, which is why O'Neill, Tennessee Williams, Arthur Miller, Albee, and August Wilson, plus more than a dozen individual plays, all clear the frequency threshold. And the category's center of gravity sits later than British Literature's: modern and contemporary writers such as David Foster Wallace, August Wilson, and Toni Morrison are asked about as often as the nineteenth-century giants, so a study plan weighted purely toward the older canon will miss a large share of real questions.

sections.name: Colonial Literature and the American Renaissance

entries.note: Puritan poet, the first published poet of the American colonies; questions cite The Tenth Muse Lately Sprung Up in America, "To My Dear and Loving Husband," and "Upon the Burning of Our House."
African-born enslaved poet of revolutionary Boston, the first African American to publish a poetry book; clues cite Poems on Various Subjects and "On Being Brought from Africa to America."
Early national sketch writer behind "Rip Van Winkle" and "The Legend of Sleepy Hollow" from The Sketch Book; also wrote A History of New York as Diedrich Knickerbocker.
Author of the Leatherstocking Tales starring Natty Bumppo — The Last of the Mohicans, The Deerslayer, The Pioneers; Twain's essay on his "literary offenses" is a stock clue.
The most famous Fireside Poet: The Song of Hiawatha, Evangeline, "Paul Revere's Ride," and "A Psalm of Life" supply most clues.
Early Romantic poet and longtime New York editor; "Thanatopsis," drafted in his teens, and "To a Waterfowl" are the near-universal clues.
Bryant's meditation on death urging readers to join the "innumerable caravan" and lie down "to pleasant dreams"; its Greek title means a view or contemplation of death.
Quaker abolitionist Fireside Poet; the winter idyll Snow-Bound anchors nearly every question, with "Barbara Frietchie" and "Maud Muller" as supporting clues.
Central Transcendentalist essayist; "Self-Reliance," Nature with its "transparent eyeball," "The American Scholar," and the Divinity School Address dominate clues.
Transcendentalist who lived at Walden Pond; Walden and "Civil Disobedience" provide nearly all clues, often alongside his night in jail and Emerson's patronage.
Dark romancer of Puritan New England: The Scarlet Letter, The House of the Seven Gables, and tales like "Young Goodman Brown," "The Minister's Black Veil," and "Rappaccini's Daughter."
Hawthorne's novel of Hester Prynne, Pearl, Dimmesdale, and Chillingworth in Puritan Boston; the three scaffold scenes and "The Custom-House" introduction recur in clues.
Author of Moby-Dick, Billy Budd, "Bartleby, the Scrivener," and "Benito Cereno"; clues range from the early Polynesian novels Typee and Omoo to The Confidence-Man.
Melville's whaling epic narrated by Ishmael; Ahab, Queequeg, Starbuck, the Pequod, and chapters like "The Whiteness of the Whale" are stock clues.
Master of Gothic tales and musical verse: "The Raven," "The Tell-Tale Heart," "The Fall of the House of Usher," and "The Cask of Amontillado," plus detective Auguste Dupin.
Free-verse poet of Leaves of Grass; "Song of Myself," the Lincoln elegies "O Captain! My Captain!" and "When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd," and "Crossing Brooklyn Ferry" recur.
Whitman's lifelong, endlessly revised collection, first published in 1855 with a preface calling the United States "essentially the greatest poem"; contains "Song of Myself" and the Calamus poems.
Fifty-two-section centerpiece of Leaves of Grass; "I celebrate myself, and sing myself," the child asking "What is the grass?", and the "barbaric yawp" are giveaway lines.
Whitman poem cataloguing the human body and including a slave-auction scene; Ray Bradbury reused the title for a story and collection, which questions often note.

The most-asked answer in American literature: the reclusive Amherst poet of "Because I could not stop for Death," "I heard a Fly buzz," and "Hope is the thing with feathers," identified by dashes and slant rhyme.
Abolitionist author of Uncle Tom's Cabin, the century's best-selling novel; clues add A Key to Uncle Tom's Cabin, Dred, and Oldtown Folks.
Stowe's 1852 anti-slavery novel: Tom, Little Eva, Eliza crossing the icy Ohio, Topsy, and the brutal overseer Simon Legree are the standard clues.
Author of Little Women and its sequels; questions cite the four March sisters, Hospital Sketches, and the sensation fiction she published as A. M. Barnard.
Samuel Clemens, humorist of the Mississippi: Adventures of Huckleberry Finn, Tom Sawyer, "The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County," A Connecticut Yankee, and Pudd'nhead Wilson.
Twain's novel of Huck and Jim rafting down the Mississippi; the Duke and the Dauphin, the Grangerford feud, and "All right, then, I'll go to hell" recur as clues.
The enslaved man who escapes down the Mississippi with Huck Finn; Percival Everett's 2024 retelling James has made him an especially current character answerline.
Expatriate master of the psychological novel: The Portrait of a Lady, The Turn of the Screw, The Ambassadors, Daisy Miller, and The Wings of the Dove.
As a bare answerline this has usually meant Henry James via surname-accepting clues, but since 2024 it may also be Percival Everett's Booker-winning novel James — check the clues.
The "Dean of American Letters," realist novelist and Atlantic Monthly editor; The Rise of Silas Lapham and A Hazard of New Fortunes are the core clues.
Novelist of Old New York society: The House of Mirth, The Age of Innocence with Newland Archer and Ellen Olenska, and the bleak New England novella Ethan Frome.
Wharton's novel of Lily Bart's social fall in New York, ending with her chloral overdose; Lawrence Selden and the tableaux vivants scene are common clues.
Louisiana local colorist; The Awakening plus the stories "The Story of an Hour" and "Désirée's Baby" make her a fixture at every difficulty.
Chopin's 1899 novel of Edna Pontellier's sexual and artistic awakening at Grand Isle, ending as she swims out into the Gulf; Robert Lebrun and Mademoiselle Reisz appear in clues.
Naturalist who died at twenty-eight: The Red Badge of Courage, Maggie: A Girl of the Streets, "The Open Boat," and the poetry collection War Is Kind.
Crane's Civil War novel of Henry Fleming, who flees his first battle and envies the wounded their "red badge"; the tall soldier Jim Conklin's death is a stock clue.
Naturalist of Sister Carrie, tracing Carrie Meeber's rise and Hurstwood's ruin, and An American Tragedy, in which Clyde Griffiths lets Roberta Alden drown.
Klondike naturalist: The Call of the Wild's dog Buck, White Fang, "To Build a Fire," and The Sea-Wolf's captain Wolf Larsen dominate questions.
Sardonic Civil War veteran: "An Occurrence at Owl Creek Bridge," "Chickamauga," and The Devil's Dictionary; his 1913 disappearance into revolutionary Mexico is a favorite clue.
Local colorist of Gold Rush California; "The Luck of Roaring Camp" and "The Outcasts of Poker Flat" provide nearly every clue.
Pen name of William Sydney Porter, the twist-ending story writer: "The Gift of the Magi," "The Ransom of Red Chief," and "The Cop and the Anthem"; he began writing in prison.
Maine regionalist of The Country of the Pointed Firs and "A White Heron," in which young Sylvia refuses to reveal the heron's nest to a visiting ornithologist.
Feminist writer of "The Yellow Wallpaper" and the all-female utopia Herland; her treatise Women and Economics also surfaces in Social Science questions.
Gilman's story of a woman confined for a "rest cure" who comes to see a creeping woman trapped behind the wallpaper; a protest against the treatment prescribed by Silas Weir Mitchell.
Novelist of the Nebraska prairie and the Southwest: My Ántonia, O Pioneers!, The Song of the Lark, and Death Comes for the Archbishop.
First of Cather's prairie trilogy: Alexandra Bergson builds up the Nebraska farm, while her brother Emil and Marie Shabata are shot under a mulberry tree by Marie's husband Frank.

sections.name: Modernist Fiction and the Lost Generation

entries.note: Iceberg-theory stylist: The Sun Also Rises, A Farewell to Arms, For Whom the Bell Tolls, The Old Man and the Sea, and stories like "Hills Like White Elephants" and "The Killers."
Jazz Age chronicler: The Great Gatsby, Tender Is the Night, This Side of Paradise, and stories including "The Curious Case of Benjamin Button" and "Babylon Revisited."
Fitzgerald's novel narrated by Nick Carraway: the green light, the eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg over the valley of ashes, and Myrtle's death under Daisy's driving are giveaways.
Expatriate salon host who told Hemingway "you are all a lost generation"; The Autobiography of Alice B. Toklas, Tender Buttons, Three Lives, and "a rose is a rose is a rose" supply clues.
Chronicler of Depression-era California: The Grapes of Wrath, Of Mice and Men with George and Lennie, East of Eden, and Cannery Row.
Steinbeck's novel of the Joads' migration from the Oklahoma Dust Bowl to California; Tom Joad's "I'll be there" speech, ex-preacher Jim Casy, and Rose of Sharon's final gesture are giveaways.
Steinbeck's Salinas Valley retelling of Cain and Abel across the Trask family; the Hebrew word timshel ("thou mayest") and the villainous Cathy Ames anchor clues.
First American winner of the literature Nobel; his satires of Midwestern conformity — Main Street, Babbitt, Arrowsmith, Elmer Gantry, and It Can't Happen Here — supply the clues.
Modernist of the U.S.A. trilogy, famous for its Newsreel and Camera Eye collage sections; Manhattan Transfer and Three Soldiers also appear.
Darkly comic novelist of Miss Lonelyhearts, about an advice columnist, and The Day of the Locust, whose Hollywood mob riot and painting "The Burning of Los Angeles" are stock clues.
Sherwood Anderson's linked story cycle about small-town "grotesques," framed by "The Book of the Grotesque" and unified by young reporter George Willard; "Hands" opens the cycle.
Algonquin Round Table wit; the story "Big Blonde," the poem "Résumé" ("you might as well live"), and her acid Constant Reader reviews fill questions.
New England poet of "The Road Not Taken," "Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening," "Mending Wall," "Birches," and "Fire and Ice"; recited at Kennedy's inauguration.
Hartford insurance executive; "The Emperor of Ice-Cream," "Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird," "Anecdote of the Jar," "Sunday Morning," and the collection Harmonium recur constantly.
Stevens poem in which a woman lounging with coffee and oranges skips church; it ends with "casual flocks of pigeons" sinking "downward to darkness, on extended wings."
Rutherford physician-poet of "The Red Wheelbarrow," "This Is Just to Say," Spring and All, and the epic Paterson; his slogan was "no ideas but in things."
Imagist impresario who edited The Waste Land: "In a Station of the Metro," Hugh Selwyn Mauberley, and The Cantos; his fascist broadcasts and confinement at St. Elizabeths recur.
Pound's sprawling, unfinished 116-plus-section epic, opening with a translation of Odysseus's descent to the underworld; includes the usura canto and the Pisan Cantos, written in a detention camp.
Pound-launched movement demanding "direct treatment of the thing"; anthologized in Des Imagistes and later led by Amy Lowell ("Amygism"); H.D. and "In a Station of the Metro" are stock clues.
Pen name of Hilda Doolittle, signed "H.D. Imagiste" by Pound; crystalline lyrics like "Oread" and "Sea Rose" plus the later epics Trilogy and Helen in Egypt appear.
Precise syllabic-verse modernist; "Poetry" ("imaginary gardens with real toads in them"), "The Fish," and her tricorn hat and Brooklyn Dodgers fandom are clues.
Visionary poet of The Bridge, addressed to the Brooklyn Bridge, and White Buildings; died jumping from the steamship Orizaba while returning from Mexico.
Lowercase typographical experimenter: "anyone lived in a pretty how town," "in Just-," "l(a," and the World War I internment memoir The Enormous Room.
Sonneteer of Greenwich Village bohemia: "First Fig" ("My candle burns at both ends"), "Renascence," and the Pulitzer-winning The Ballad of the Harp-Weaver.
British-born poet who became a U.S. citizen and is asked as American literature: "Musée des Beaux Arts," "Funeral Blues," "The Unknown Citizen," and The Age of Anxiety.
Auden's poem written "in one of the dives / on Fifty-second Street" at the outbreak of World War II; "We must love one another or die" is the famous line he later disowned.
Poet of fictional Tilbury Town: "Richard Cory," who "went home and put a bullet through his head," plus "Miniver Cheevy" and "Mr. Flood's Party."
Performer of chanted "singing poetry": "The Congo," "General William Booth Enters into Heaven," and "Abraham Lincoln Walks at Midnight"; he discovered Langston Hughes waiting tables.
Author of the Pulitzer-winning Civil War verse epic John Brown's Body and the story "The Devil and Daniel Webster," in which Webster out-argues Mr. Scratch before a jury of the damned.
Nobel laureate of fictional Yoknapatawpha County: The Sound and the Fury, As I Lay Dying, Absalom, Absalom!, Light in August, and "A Rose for Emily."
Faulkner's Compson family novel in four sections — Benjy's, Quentin's (ending in suicide at Harvard), Jason's, and Dilsey's; the title comes from Macbeth.
Georgia Catholic master of the grotesque: "A Good Man Is Hard to Find" with The Misfit, "Good Country People," Wise Blood, and The Violent Bear It Away.
O'Connor story in which Julian's mother collapses after a Black woman wearing the same hat strikes her at a bus stop; the title comes from Teilhard de Chardin.
Mississippi story writer: "A Worn Path" with Phoenix Jackson, "Why I Live at the P.O.," the Pulitzer-winning The Optimist's Daughter, and the memoir One Writer's Beginnings.
Georgia novelist of lonely misfits: The Heart Is a Lonely Hunter, The Member of the Wedding with Frankie Addams, and The Ballad of the Sad Café.

entries.topic: The Heart Is a Lonely Hunter
entries.answerline: The Heart is a Lonely Hunter
entries.note: McCullers's debut about deaf-mute John Singer, confidant of Mick Kelly, Dr. Copeland, Jake Blount, and Biff Brannon in a Georgia mill town; Singer shoots himself after his friend Antonapoulos dies.
Texas-born writer of Pale Horse, Pale Rider, "The Jilting of Granny Weatherall," the Miranda stories, and the allegorical ocean-liner novel Ship of Fools.
The only writer with Pulitzers in both fiction and poetry, and the first official U.S. Poet Laureate; All the King's Men and the Fugitive-Agrarian circle supply the clues.
Warren's novel of Louisiana demagogue Willie Stark, narrated by Jack Burden, whose historical digging drives Judge Irwin to suicide; loosely based on Huey Long.
Author of the "nonfiction novel" In Cold Blood, about the Clutter family murders, plus Breakfast at Tiffany's with Holly Golightly and Other Voices, Other Rooms.
Novelist of violent Souths and Southwests: Blood Meridian with Judge Holden, the Border Trilogy beginning with All the Pretty Horses, No Country for Old Men, and The Road.
McCarthy's post-apocalyptic Pulitzer winner following an unnamed father and son "carrying the fire" toward the coast, pushing a shopping cart through the ash.
John Kennedy Toole's posthumous Pulitzer-winning New Orleans comedy starring hot-dog vendor Ignatius J. Reilly, devotee of Boethius; published through Walker Percy's advocacy.

sections.name: Harlem Renaissance and African American Literature

entries.note: Escaped slave, orator, and editor of The North Star; his 1845 Narrative, the fight with slave-breaker Covey, and "What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?" are the clues.
First nationally famous Black American poet; "We Wear the Mask" and "Sympathy" ("I know why the caged bird sings") anchor nearly every question.
Writer of "Lift Every Voice and Sing," the novel The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man, and the sermon-poems of God's Trombones; also an NAACP leader and diplomat.
The 1920s flowering of Black art centered in upper Manhattan: Hughes, Hurston, McKay, Countee Cullen, and Alain Locke's anthology The New Negro are the standard clues.
Jamaican-born Harlem Renaissance poet of the defiant sonnet "If We Must Die" and "America" ("this cultured hell that tests my youth"), plus the novel Home to Harlem.
Poet laureate of the Harlem Renaissance: "The Negro Speaks of Rivers," "Harlem," "I, Too," "The Weary Blues," and the Jesse B. Semple ("Simple") stories.
Usually Hughes's poem asking "What happens to a dream deferred?" — "does it dry up / like a raisin in the sun?" — from Montage of a Dream Deferred; sometimes the neighborhood as a common link.
Anthropologist-novelist from Eatonville, Florida: Their Eyes Were Watching God, the folklore collection Mules and Men, and the essay "How It Feels to Be Colored Me."
Hurston's novel of Janie Crawford's three marriages; the pear tree, the mule of Eatonville, the hurricane, and the shooting of rabid Tea Cake are the standard clues.
Naturalist of Black protest fiction: Native Son, the memoir Black Boy, and the story collection Uncle Tom's Children with "Big Boy Leaves Home."
Wright's novel of Bigger Thomas, who accidentally smothers Mary Dalton in Chicago and is defended by Boris Max; its three books are Fear, Flight, and Fate.
Ralph Ellison's 1952 novel of an unnamed narrator's journey from a Southern "battle royal" through the Brotherhood in Harlem, ending underground in a room lit by 1,369 bulbs.
Essayist-novelist of Go Tell It on the Mountain, Giovanni's Room, and The Fire Next Time; the story "Sonny's Blues" is a stock short-fiction clue.
First Black Pulitzer winner, for Annie Allen; "We Real Cool" ("We / Die soon") and the Chicago world of A Street in Bronzeville dominate clues.
First Black poet to hold the Library of Congress poetry consultantship; "Those Winter Sundays" ("love's austere and lonely offices") and the slave-ship poem "Middle Passage" are the clues.
Memoirist-poet of I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings; "Still I Rise," "Phenomenal Woman," and reading "On the Pulse of Morning" at Clinton's inauguration recur.
Black Arts Movement founder, formerly LeRoi Jones: the play Dutchman, in which Lula murders Clay on a subway, and the controversial poem "Somebody Blew Up America."
Self-described "Black, lesbian, mother, warrior, poet"; the essay "The Master's Tools Will Never Dismantle the Master's House," The Cancer Journals, and the "biomythography" Zami.
Author of The Color Purple; the story "Everyday Use," the essays of In Search of Our Mothers' Gardens, and her role reviving Hurston's reputation also appear.
Walker's epistolary Pulitzer winner: Celie's letters to God and from her missionary sister Nettie in Africa, with blues singer Shug Avery and the abusive Mister.
Nobel laureate whose Beloved, Song of Solomon, The Bluest Eye, Sula, and Jazz make her the most-asked postwar American novelist.
Morrison's novel of Sethe, haunted at 124 Bluestone Road by the daughter she killed rather than return to slavery; Paul D, Denver, and the Sweet Home plantation are stock clues.
Morrison's novel of Milkman Dead's journey south to trace the flying African Solomon; his aunt Pilate, born without a navel, and the insurance agent's opening leap are clues.
Morrison's first novel: Pecola Breedlove prays for blue eyes in Lorain, Ohio; partly narrated by Claudia MacTeer and framed by a distorted Dick-and-Jane primer.
Back-to-back Pulitzer winner for The Underground Railroad, which makes the railroad literal, and The Nickel Boys, based on the Dozier School; earlier wrote The Intuitionist and Zone One.
America's first great playwright and its only dramatist Nobel laureate: Long Day's Journey into Night, The Iceman Cometh, The Hairy Ape, Desire Under the Elms, and Mourning Becomes Electra.
O'Neill play set in Harry Hope's saloon, where salesman Hickey urges the regulars to abandon their "pipe dreams"; he is revealed to have murdered his wife Evelyn.
O'Neill's posthumously produced autobiographical play: miserly actor James Tyrone, his morphine-addicted wife Mary, and sons Jamie and consumptive Edmund across one day in Connecticut.
Playwright-novelist: Our Town, The Skin of Our Teeth, and The Matchmaker (source of Hello, Dolly!), plus the Pulitzer-winning novel The Bridge of San Luis Rey.
Wilder's play narrated by the Stage Manager in Grover's Corners, New Hampshire; Emily Webb marries George Gibbs, dies, and relives her twelfth birthday from the cemetery.
Wilder's fourth-wall-breaking allegory of the Antrobus family of Excelsior, New Jersey, who survive an ice age, a flood, and war alongside the maid Sabina.
Group Theatre social dramatist of the Depression: Waiting for Lefty, Awake and Sing!, and Golden Boy, about violinist-turned-boxer Joe Bonaparte.
Odets's agitprop one-act at a taxi-union meeting awaiting organizer Lefty Costello, who is found shot dead; it ends with the audience-rousing cry of "Strike!"
Playwright of The Little Foxes, The Children's Hour, and Watch on the Rhine; her memoir Pentimento and her defiant letter to HUAC also appear as clues.
Hellman's play about the grasping Hubbard family of the post-Reconstruction South; Regina Giddens lets her husband Horace die by withholding his heart medicine.
Hellman play in which student Mary Tilford's lie that teachers Karen and Martha are lovers destroys their school; Martha confesses her feelings and kills herself.
Southern lyric dramatist: A Streetcar Named Desire, The Glass Menagerie, Cat on a Hot Tin Roof, Suddenly Last Summer, and The Night of the Iguana.
Williams's "memory play": narrator Tom Wingfield, his mother Amanda, his fragile sister Laura with her glass unicorn, and the gentleman caller Jim O'Connor.
Williams play in which Blanche DuBois arrives at Stella and Stanley Kowalski's Elysian Fields apartment; "STELLA!", the paper lantern, and "the kindness of strangers" are giveaways.

entries.topic: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof
entries.answerline: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof
entries.note: Williams play set at Big Daddy Pollitt's birthday on a Mississippi Delta plantation; alcoholic Brick, his wife Maggie "the Cat," and the truth about Skipper drive the plot.
Playwright of Death of a Salesman, The Crucible, All My Sons, and A View from the Bridge; his marriage to Marilyn Monroe and HUAC testimony appear as biographical clues.
Miller's tragedy of Willy Loman and his sons Biff and Happy; "attention must be paid," the rubber hose, and the Requiem's "We're free and clear" are stock clues.
Miller's Salem witch-trial allegory of McCarthyism: John Proctor's cry "Because it is my name!", the accuser Abigail Williams, and Giles Corey pressed to death demanding "more weight."
Miller play about Joe Keller, who shipped cracked cylinder heads that killed twenty-one pilots; his son Larry's suicide letter forces the recognition that "they were all my sons."
First Black woman to have a play on Broadway, with A Raisin in the Sun; The Sign in Sidney Brustein's Window and To Be Young, Gifted and Black also appear.
Hansberry's play about the Youngers' insurance money and move to white Clybourne Park: Walter Lee's liquor-store dream, Beneatha, Mama's plant, and Karl Lindner's buyout offer.
Playwright bridging absurdism and domestic drama: Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?, The Zoo Story, A Delicate Balance, and The Goat, or Who Is Sylvia?
Albee's play of George and Martha's all-night "Fun and Games" with guests Nick and Honey, climaxing in the "killing" of George and Martha's imaginary son.
Albee's one-act on a Central Park bench: drifter Jerry tells the "story of Jerry and the dog," provokes publisher Peter, and impales himself on the knife he makes Peter hold.
Prolific Broadway comedy writer: The Odd Couple's Oscar and Felix, Barefoot in the Park, the autobiographical Brighton Beach trilogy, and the Pulitzer-winning Lost in Yonkers.
Playwright of clipped, profane "Mamet speak": Glengarry Glen Ross, American Buffalo, Oleanna, and Speed-the-Plow.
Mamet's Pulitzer winner about desperate Chicago real-estate salesmen — Shelly Levene, Ricky Roma, the stolen Glengarry leads, and the film's added "Always be closing" speech.
Author of the ten-play Pittsburgh Cycle chronicling each decade of twentieth-century Black life: Fences, The Piano Lesson, Ma Rainey's Black Bottom, and Joe Turner's Come and Gone.
Wilson's Pulitzer winner about ex-Negro Leagues slugger Troy Maxson, who builds a fence, taunts Death, and blocks his son Cory's football scholarship; Gabriel opens the gates at the end.
Wilson play in which Boy Willie wants to sell the piano carved with the family's history, which his sister Berniece refuses to touch; it ends with the exorcism of Sutter's ghost.
Tony Kushner's two-part epic of the 1980s AIDS crisis: prophet-despite-himself Prior Walter, closeted Joe Pitt, Roy Cohn, and the Angel crashing through the ceiling.

variants.answerline: Angels in America: A Gay Fantasia on National Themes

entries.note: The first woman to win two drama Pulitzers: Ruined, set in a bar-brothel in wartime Congo, and Sweat, about factory workers in Reading, Pennsylvania.
Meticulous poet of "One Art" ("the art of losing isn't hard to master"), "The Fish," "In the Waiting Room," and "The Moose"; lived for years in Brazil with Lota de Macedo Soares.
Founder of confessional poetry with Life Studies; "Skunk Hour," "For the Union Dead," and "Memories of West Street and Lepke," from his conscientious-objector jail term, recur.
Confessional poet of Ariel — "Daddy," "Lady Lazarus" ("I eat men like air") — and The Colossus; The Bell Jar and her marriage to Ted Hughes appear constantly.
Plath's autobiographical novel of Esther Greenwood's New York magazine internship, breakdown, and shock treatment; first published under the pseudonym Victoria Lucas.
Confessional poet who began writing in therapy: To Bedlam and Part Way Back, the Pulitzer-winning Live or Die, and the fairy-tale revisions of Transformations.
Confessional poet of The Dream Songs, starring Henry and the minstrel-voiced "Mr. Bones," and Homage to Mistress Bradstreet; he jumped from a Minneapolis bridge.
Poet-critic best known for the five-line "The Death of the Ball Turret Gunner," which ends "they washed me out of the turret with a hose"; also wrote Pictures from an Institution.
Movement label for intensely autobiographical postwar verse; Lowell's Life Studies, Plath, Sexton, Berryman, and W. D. Snodgrass's Heart's Needle are the canonical clues.
Beat poet of "Howl," "America," and "Kaddish," his elegy for his mother Naomi; "A Supermarket in California" imagines meeting Whitman among the groceries.

Ginsberg's Beat epic beginning "I saw the best minds of my generation destroyed by madness"; addresses Carl Solomon ("I'm with you in Rockland"), rails against Moloch, and survived a 1957 obscenity trial.

Ginsberg poem addressing the country directly — "America I've given you all and now I'm nothing" — and ending "I'm putting my queer shoulder to the wheel"; Claude McKay's sonnet of the same title also feeds clues.
1950s counterculture circle of Ginsberg, Kerouac, Burroughs, and Neal Cassady, centered on City Lights bookstore and the Six Gallery reading; Kerouac coined the name.
Beat novelist of On the Road, typed on a continuous scroll, with Sal Paradise and Dean Moriarty; The Dharma Bums and Big Sur also appear.
Beat author of the cut-up novel Naked Lunch, plus Junky and Queer; he killed his wife Joan Vollmer in a drunken "William Tell" act in Mexico City.
Skid-row poet-novelist of Los Angeles and his alter ego Henry Chinaski: Post Office, Ham on Rye, Factotum, and thousands of plainspoken poems.
New York School poet and MoMA curator; Lunch Poems pieces like "The Day Lady Died," on Billie Holiday's death, and "Why I Am Not a Painter" recur.
New York School poet whose Self-Portrait in a Convex Mirror, on Parmigianino's painting, swept the Pulitzer, National Book Award, and NBCC; famously difficult and prolific.
Feminist poet of "Diving into the Wreck," "Aunt Jennifer's Tigers," and Snapshots of a Daughter-in-Law; she refused the National Medal of Arts in 1997.
Muscogee (Creek) poet and the first Native American U.S. Poet Laureate; "She Had Some Horses," An American Sunrise, and "Perhaps the World Ends Here" are common clues.
Reclusive author of The Catcher in the Rye and the Glass family stories — Franny and Zooey and "A Perfect Day for Bananafish," which ends in Seymour's suicide.
Suffixed answerline form of Kurt Vonnegut; clues cover Billy Pilgrim coming "unstuck in time," recurring author-avatar Kilgore Trout, and the Tralfamadorians.
Vonnegut novel of ice-nine, which freezes the world's water; narrator John/Jonah, the invented religion of Bokononism, and the island of San Lorenzo are the clues.
Joseph Heller's World War II satire of bombardier Yossarian on Pianosa; the title's circular logic about flying missions, Milo Minderbinder's syndicate, and Snowden's death recur.

entries.topic: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
entries.answerline: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
entries.note: Ken Kesey's asylum novel narrated by Chief Bromden: gambler Randle McMurphy battles Nurse Ratched, is lobotomized, and is smothered by the Chief, who escapes.
Nobel-winning novelist of Jewish Chicago: The Adventures of Augie March, Herzog with its unsent letters, Humboldt's Gift, Seize the Day, and Henderson the Rain King.
Chronicler of Jewish Newark and alter ego Nathan Zuckerman: Portnoy's Complaint, American Pastoral, The Human Stain, Goodbye, Columbus, and The Plot Against America.
Roth's 1969 succès de scandale: Alexander Portnoy's unbroken monologue to the psychoanalyst Dr. Spielvogel about his mother Sophie and his compulsions; the liver scene is infamous.
New Journalism brawler: The Naked and the Dead, the Pulitzer-winning The Armies of the Night, and The Executioner's Song, about Gary Gilmore's execution.
"Chekhov of the suburbs": stories like "The Swimmer," "The Enormous Radio," and "The Five-Forty-Eight," plus the Wapshot novels and the prison novel Falconer.
Cheever story in which Neddy Merrill swims home across the county through the "Lucinda River" of backyard pools, arriving at an empty, locked house as the seasons inexplicably turn.
Lyricist of suburban adultery: the four Rabbit Angstrom novels, the story "A&P" narrated by the checkout boy Sammy, and The Witches of Eastwick.
Minimalist "dirty realist" story writer, heavily edited by Gordon Lish: "Cathedral," "What We Talk About When We Talk About Love," and "A Small, Good Thing."

entries.topic: What We Talk About When We Talk About Love
entries.answerline: "What We Talk About When We Talk About Love"
entries.note: Carver's title story: two couples — cardiologist Mel and Terri, Nick and Laura — drink gin and argue about love, including the old couple injured in a car crash.
Gothic writer of "The Lottery," which drew furious New Yorker mail over Tessie Hutchinson's stoning, The Haunting of Hill House, and We Have Always Lived in the Castle.
Hyper-prolific novelist; "Where Are You Going, Where Have You Been?" with Arnold Friend, the National Book Award winner them, and Blonde are the usual clues.
Chicago novelist of the lower depths: The Man with the Golden Arm, about morphine-addicted card dealer Frankie Machine, and A Walk on the Wild Side; Simone de Beauvoir's lover.
Science-fiction lyricist: Fahrenheit 451 with fireman Guy Montag, The Martian Chronicles, Something Wicked This Way Comes, and Dandelion Wine.
Prolific SF grandmaster: the Foundation series built on Hari Seldon's psychohistory, the Three Laws of Robotics from I, Robot, and the story "Nightfall."
Master of anthropological SF and fantasy: The Left Hand of Darkness, The Dispossessed, the Earthsea books, and "The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas."
Nabokov's novel narrated by Humbert Humbert ("light of my life, fire of my loins"): his obsession with Dolores Haze, the rival Clare Quilty, and the cross-country motel odyssey.
Reclusive postmodernist: Gravity's Rainbow, The Crying of Lot 49, V., Mason & Dixon, and Inherent Vice; entropy and paranoia are his signature clue-words.
Pynchon's V-2 rocket epic opening "A screaming comes across the sky": Tyrone Slothrop's conditioned map of London, Rocket 00000, and the postwar Zone.
Pynchon novella in which Oedipa Maas, executing Pierce Inverarity's estate, uncovers the Trystero and the muted post horn of the underground mail system W.A.S.T.E.
Postmodernist of White Noise, Underworld — which opens at the "Shot Heard 'Round the World" baseball game — Libra, and Mao II.
DeLillo's National Book Award-winning satire of Hitler-studies professor Jack Gladney, the Airborne Toxic Event, and the fear-of-death drug Dylar.
Author of Infinite Jest, the cruise-ship essay "A Supposedly Fun Thing I'll Never Do Again," the commencement speech "This Is Water," and the unfinished The Pale King.
Wallace's mammoth novel set at Enfield Tennis Academy and Ennet House: the Incandenza family, wheelchair-bound Quebecois separatists, and the lethally entertaining "samizdat" film.
E. L. Doctorow's novel weaving Coalhouse Walker Jr.'s Model T revenge plot with historical figures — Houdini, Emma Goldman, J. P. Morgan — in 1900s New York.
White-suited New Journalist: The Electric Kool-Aid Acid Test, The Right Stuff, and the novel The Bonfire of the Vanities with Sherman McCoy, self-styled "Master of the Universe."
Essayist of California unraveling: Slouching Towards Bethlehem, The White Album, the novel Play It as It Lays, and the grief memoir The Year of Magical Thinking.
Essayist-critic of "Notes on 'Camp'," Against Interpretation, On Photography, and Illness as Metaphor; her novel The Volcano Lover also appears.
Pulitzer winner for The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier & Clay, about Golden Age comic-book creators; The Yiddish Policemen's Union and The Mysteries of Pittsburgh also come up.
Novelist of Midwestern family sagas: The Corrections, about the Lamberts, and Freedom; his Oprah's Book Club snub and the essay "Perchance to Dream" appear as clues.
Chronicler of Bengali American life: the Pulitzer-winning Interpreter of Maladies, The Namesake with Gogol Ganguli, and later books written in Italian.
Satirical story writer of Tenth of December and CivilWarLand in Bad Decline; won the Booker Prize for Lincoln in the Bardo, narrated by graveyard ghosts.
Spokane/Coeur d'Alene writer of The Lone Ranger and Tonto Fistfight in Heaven, the basis for the film Smoke Signals, and The Absolutely True Diary of a Part-Time Indian.
Amy Tan's novel of four Chinese immigrant mothers and their American-born daughters trading stories over mahjong; Jing-mei "June" Woo travels to China to meet her half-sisters.
Ubiquitous common link: Whitman's ferries, Hart Crane's bridge, the Harlem Renaissance, Bartleby's Wall Street, and Gatsby's West Egg parties all feed clues.
Common link via Steinbeck's valleys, Didion's essays, Bret Harte's mining camps, the Los Angeles of Chandler and Nathanael West, and "A Supermarket in California."
Common link anchored by Tim O'Brien's The Things They Carried and Going After Cacciato, with veteran poets like Yusef Komunyakaa ("Facing It") close behind.
Personified common link: Dickinson's kindly stopping carriage driver, Whitman's dark mother in "Lilacs," and Fences' Troy Maxson wrestling Death, plus countless title clues.
Common link: The Red Badge of Courage, Whitman's Drum-Taps and hospital service, Benét's John Brown's Body, "An Occurrence at Owl Creek Bridge," and Cold Mountain.

Literary common link: Whitman's elegies "O Captain! My Captain!" and "When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd," Sandburg's multivolume biography, Lindsay's "Abraham Lincoln Walks at Midnight," and Saunders's Lincoln in the Bardo.
Common link on the abolitionist in literature: Benét's epic John Brown's Body, Thoreau's "A Plea for Captain John Brown," and James McBride's The Good Lord Bird.
Expatriate common link: Hemingway's A Moveable Feast, Stein's salon at 27 rue de Fleurus, Baldwin's Giovanni's Room, and Henry Miller's Tropic of Cancer.
Common link: Sandburg's "Hog Butcher for the World" poem, Sister Carrie, Native Son, The Adventures of Augie March, A Raisin in the Sun, and Upton Sinclair's The Jungle.
Common link: the Beats at City Lights and the Six Gallery "Howl" reading, Amy Tan's Chinatown, Hammett's The Maltese Falcon, and Kerouac's Big Sur trips.
Common link built on August Wilson's ten-play cycle in the Hill District, plus Chabon's The Mysteries of Pittsburgh, Cather's "Paul's Case," and Annie Dillard's An American Childhood.
Common link: Puritan Boston in The Scarlet Letter, Dickinson's Amherst, Thoreau's Walden Pond, Lowell's "For the Union Dead," and the Salem of The Crucible.
Common link covering both state and river: Faulkner's Yoknapatawpha, home state of Welty and Wright, and Twain's Life on the Mississippi and Huck's raft.
Common link as a destination of escape: fugitive slaves head there in Uncle Tom's Cabin and Beloved, and it recurs as a border-crossing setting and refuge across American fiction.
Common link led by Pearl S. Buck's The Good Earth and her Nobel Prize, plus Amy Tan's mother-daughter novels and Maxine Hong Kingston's The Woman Warrior.
The magazine as an answerline: it published "The Lottery" to record hate mail, ran Salinger, Cheever, and Updike's stories, devoted an issue to Hersey's "Hiroshima," and employed Parker and Thurber.
Common link: Malamud's The Natural, Thayer's "Casey at the Bat," the Polo Grounds prologue of DeLillo's Underworld, Roth's The Great American Novel, and Kinsella's Shoeless Joe.
Genre common link: Franklin's Autobiography, The Autobiography of Malcolm X, Stein's The Autobiography of Alice B. Toklas, and Johnson's The Autobiography of an Ex-Colored Man.
Common link: Poe's raven, Dickinson's "Hope is the thing with feathers," Jewett's white heron, the caged bird of Dunbar and Angelou, and Whitman's mockingbird.

First-name common link spanning many authors and characters named John; answerlines this bare usually come from questions accepting any "John," so treat it as a grab-bag rather than one canonical topic.
First-name common link — Frost, Lowell, Penn Warren, and Hayden among poets alone; like "John," it reflects shared-name common-link questions rather than a single canonical answer.
